title: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## FINETUNED LANGUAGE MODELS ARE ZERO-SHOT LEARNERS

## **Jason Wei** _[∗]_ **, Maarten Bosma** _[∗]_ **, Vincent Y. Zhao** _[∗]_ **, Kelvin Guu** _[∗]_ **, Adams Wei Yu, Brian Lester, Nan Du, Andrew M. Dai, and Quoc V. Le**

Google Research

## ABSTRACT

This paper explores a simple method for improving the zero-shot learning abilities of language models. We show that _instruction tuning_ —finetuning language models on a collection of datasets described via instructions—substantially improves zeroshot performance on unseen tasks.

We take a 137B parameter pretrained language model and instruction tune it on over 60 NLP datasets verbalized via natural language instruction templates. We evaluate this instruction-tuned model, which we call FLAN, on unseen task types. FLAN substantially improves the performance of its unmodified counterpart and surpasses zero-shot 175B GPT-3 on 20 of 25 datasets that we evaluate. FLAN even outperforms few-shot GPT-3 by a large margin on ANLI, RTE, BoolQ, AI2-ARC, OpenbookQA, and StoryCloze. Ablation studies reveal that number of finetuning datasets, model scale, and natural language instructions are key to the success of instruction tuning.

## **Finetune on many tasks (“instruction-tuning”)**

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Language models (LMs) at scale, such as GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), have been shown to perform few-shot learning remarkably well. They are less successful at zero-shot learning, however. For example, GPT-3’s zero-shot performance is much worse than few-shot performance on tasks such as reading comprehension, question answering, and natural language inference. One potential reason is that, without few-shot exemplars, it is harder for models to perform well on prompts that are not similar to the format of the pretraining data.

In this paper, we explore a simple method to improve the zero-shot performance of large language models, which would expand their reach to a broader audience. We leverage the intuition that NLP tasks can be described via natural language instructions, such as “ _Is the sentiment of this movie review positive or negative?_ ” or “ _Translate ‘how are you’ into Chinese._ ” We take a pretrained language model of 137B parameters and perform _instruction tuning_ —finetuning the model on a mixture of more than 60 NLP datasets expressed via natural language instructions. We refer to this resulting model as FLAN, for Finetuned Language Net.

To evaluate the zero-shot performance of FLAN on unseen tasks, we group NLP datasets into clusters based on their task types and hold out each cluster for evaluation while instruction tuning FLAN on all other clusters. For example, as shown in Figure 1, to evaluate FLAN’s ability to perform natural language inference, we instruction tune the model on a range of other NLP tasks such as commonsense reasoning, translation, and sentiment analysis. As this setup ensures that FLAN has not seen any natural language inference tasks in instruction tuning, we then evaluate its ability to perform zero-shot natural language inference.

Our evaluations show that FLAN substantially improves the zero-shot performance of the base 137B-parameter model. FLAN’s zero-shot also outperforms 175B-parameter GPT-3’s zero-shot on 20 of 25 datasets that we evaluate, and even outperforms GPT-3’s few-shot by a large margin on ANLI, RTE, BoolQ, AI2-ARC, OpenbookQA, and StoryCloze. In ablation studies, we find that increasing the number of task clusters in instruction tuning improves performance on unseen tasks and that the benefits of instruction tuning emerge only with sufficient model scale.

Instruction tuning is a simple method that, as depicted in Figure 2, combines appealing aspects of both the pretrain–finetune and prompting paradigms by using supervision via finetuning to improve language model’s responses to inference-time text interactions. Our empirical results demonstrate promising abilities of language models to perform tasks described purely via instructions. Source code for loading the instruction tuning dataset used for FLAN is publicly available at https://github.com/google-research/flan.

## 2 FLAN: INSTRUCTION TUNING IMPROVES ZERO-SHOT LEARNING

The motivation of instruction tuning is to improve the ability of language models to respond to NLP instructions. The idea is that by using supervision to teach an LM to perform tasks described via instructions, the LM will learn to follow instructions and do so even for unseen tasks. To evaluate performance on unseen tasks, we group datasets into clusters by task type and hold out each task cluster for evaluation while instruction tuning on all remaining clusters.

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 2.1 TASKS & TEMPLATES

As creating an instruction tuning dataset with many tasks from scratch would be resource-intensive, we transform existing datasets from the research community into an instructional format. We aggregate 62 text datasets that are publicly available on Tensorflow Datasets, including both language understanding and language generation tasks, into a single mixture. Figure 3 shows these datasets— each dataset is categorized into one of twelve task clusters, for which datasets in a given cluster are of the same task type. Descriptions, sizes, and examples of each dataset are shown in Appendix G.

While most of the ten templates describe the original task, to increase diversity, for each dataset we also include up to three templates that “turned the task around,” (e.g., for sentiment classification we include templates asking to generate a movie review). We then instruction tune a pretrained language model on the mixture of all datasets, with examples in each dataset formatted via a randomly selected instruction template for that dataset. ## 2.2 EVALUATION SPLITS

We are interested in how FLAN performs on tasks not seen in instruction tuning, and so it is crucial to define what counts as an unseen task. Whereas some prior work defines unseen tasks by disallowing the same dataset to appear in training, we use a more conservative definition that leverages the task clusters from Figure 3. In this work, we only consider dataset _D_ unseen at evaluation time if no datasets from any task clusters that _D_ belongs to were seen during instruction tuning. For instance, if _D_ is an entailment task, then no entailment datasets appeared in instruction tuning, and we instruction-tuned on all other clusters.[1] Hence, to evaluate zero-shot FLAN on _c_ task clusters, we instruction tune _c_ models, where each model holds out a different task cluster for evaluation.

1 When evaluating on the read. comp. with commonsense cluster, both read. comp. and commonsense reasoning were dropped from instruction tuning. Conversely, the read. comp. with commonsense cluster was not used for instruction tuning when evaluating on read. comp. or commonsense reasoning. We also drop the paraphrase cluster from instruction tuning when evaluating on NLI tasks and vice-versa.

3

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 2.3 CLASSIFICATION WITH OPTIONS

The output space for a given task is either one of several classes (classification) or free text (generation). As FLAN is an instruction-tuned version of a decoder-only language model, it naturally responds in free text, and so no further modifications are needed for generation tasks.

For classification tasks, prior work (Brown et al., 2020) used a _rank classification_ approach where, for example, only two outputs (“ _yes_ ” and “ _no_ ”) are considered and the higher probability one is taken as the model’s prediction. Though this procedure is logically sound, it is imperfect in that the probability mass for answers may have an undesired distribution among ways of saying each answer (e.g., a large number of alternative ways of saying “ _yes_ ” may lower the probability mass assigned to “ _yes_ ”). Therefore, we include an _options_ suffix, in which we append the token OPTIONS to the end of a classification task along with a list of the output classes for that task. This makes the model aware of which choices are desired when responding to classification tasks. Example use of options is shown in the NLI and commonsense examples in Figure 1.

## 2.4 TRAINING DETAILS

**Model architecture and pretraining.** In our experiments, we use LaMDA-PT, a dense left-to-right, decoder-only transformer language model of 137B parameters (Thoppilan et al., 2022). This model is pretrained on a collection of web documents (including those with computer code), dialog data, and Wikipedia, tokenized into 2.49T BPE tokens with a 32k vocabulary using the SentencePiece library (Kudo & Richardson, 2018). Around 10% of the pretraining data was non-English. Note that LaMDA-PT only has language model pretraining (c.f. LaMDA, which was finetuned for dialog).

**Instruction tuning procedure.** FLAN is the instruction-tuned version of LaMDA-PT. Our instruction tuning pipeline mixes all datasets and randomly samples from each dataset. To balance the different sizes of datasets, we limit the number of training examples per dataset to 30k and follow the examples-proportional mixing scheme (Raffel et al., 2020) with a mixing rate maximum of 3k.[2] We finetune all models for 30k gradient steps with a batch size of 8,192 tokens using the Adafactor Optimizer (Shazeer & Stern, 2018) with a learning rate of 3e-5. The input and target sequence lengths used in finetuning are 1024 and 256, respectively. We use packing (Raffel et al., 2020) to combine multiple training examples into a single sequence, separating inputs from targets using a special EOS token. This instruction tuning takes around 60 hours on a TPUv3 with 128 cores. For all evaluations, we report results on the final checkpoint trained for 30k steps.

## 3 RESULTS

We evaluate FLAN on natural language inference, reading comprehension, closed-book QA, translation, commonsense reasoning, coreference resolution, and struct-to-text. As described in §2.2, we evaluate on unseen tasks by grouping datasets into task clusters and holding out each cluster for evaluation while instruction tuning on all remaining clusters (i.e., each evaluation task cluster uses a different checkpoint). For each dataset, we evaluate the mean of performance on all templates, which proxies the expected performance given a typical natural language instruction. As a dev set is sometimes available for manual prompt engineering (Brown et al., 2020), for each dataset we also obtain the test set performance using the template with the best dev set performance.

For comparison, we report zero and few-shot results for LaMDA-PT using the same prompts as GPT-3 (as LaMDA-PT is not suitable for natural instructions without instruction tuning). This baseline provides the most direct ablation of how much instruction tuning helps. Instruction tuning significantly improves LaMDA-PT on most datasets.

We also show the zero-shot performances of GPT-3 175B (Brown et al., 2020) and GLaM 64B/64E (Du et al., 2021), as reported in their respective papers. With the best dev template, zero-shot FLAN outperforms zero-shot GPT-3 on 20 of 25 datasets and even surpasses GPT-3’s few-shot performance on 10 datasets. With the best dev-template, zero-shot FLAN outperforms zero-shot GLaM on 13 of 19 available datasets and one-shot GLaM on 11 of 19 datasets.
> 2In this mixing scheme, a mixing rate maximum of 3,000 means that a dataset does not receive additional sampling weight for examples in excess of 3,000.

4

Overall, we observe that instruction tuning is very effective on tasks naturally verbalized as instructions (e.g., NLI, QA, translation, struct-to-text) and is less effective on tasks directly formulated as language modeling, where instructions would be largely redundant (e.g., commonsense reasoning and coreference resolution tasks that are formatted as finishing an incomplete sentence or paragraph). Results on natural language inference, reading comprehension, closed-book QA, and translation are summarized in Figure 5 and described below.

Figure 5: Zero-shot performance of FLAN compared to LaMDA-PT 137B, GPT-3 175B, and GLaM 64B/64E on natural language inference, reading comprehension, closed-book QA, and translation. Performance of FLAN is the mean of up to 10 instructional templates per task. Supervised models were either T5, BERT, or translation models (specified in Table 2 and Table 1 in the Appendix).

**Natural language inference (NLI).** On five NLI datasets, where a model must determine whether a hypothesis is true given some premise, FLAN outperforms all baselines by a large margin. As noted by Brown et al. (2020), perhaps one reason why GPT-3 struggles with NLI is that NLI examples are unlikely to have appeared naturally in an unsupervised training set and are thus awkwardly phrased as a continuation of a sentence. For FLAN, we phrase NLI as the more natural question “Does <premise> mean that <hypothesis>?”, achieving much higher performance.

**Reading comprehension.** On reading comprehension, where models are asked to answer a question about a provided passage, FLAN outperforms baselines for MultiRC (Khashabi et al., 2018) and OBQA (Mihaylov et al., 2018). On BoolQ (Clark et al., 2019a), FLAN outperforms GPT-3 by a large margin, though LaMDA-PT already achieves high performance on BoolQ.

**Closed-book QA.** For closed-book QA, which asks models to answer questions about the world without access to specific information containing the answer, FLAN outperforms GPT-3 on all four datasets. Compared to GLaM, FLAN has better performance on ARC-e and ARC-c (Clark et al., 2018), and slightly lower performance on NQ (Lee et al., 2019; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019) and TQA (Joshi et al., 2017).

**Translation.** Similar to GPT-3, the training data for LaMDA-PT is around 90% English and includes some text in other languages that was not specifically used to train the model to perform machine translation. We also evaluate FLAN’s performance on machine translation for the three datasets evaluated in the GPT-3 paper: French–English from WMT’14 (Bojar et al., 2014), and German–

5

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

English and Romanian–English from WMT’16 (Bojar et al., 2016). Compared with GPT-3, FLAN outperforms zero-shot GPT-3 for all six evaluations, though it underperforms few-shot GPT-3 in most cases. Similar to GPT-3, FLAN shows strong results for translating into English and compares favorably against supervised translation baselines. Translating from English into other languages, however, was relatively weaker, as might be expected given that FLAN uses an English sentencepiece tokenizer and that the majority of pretraining data is English.

**Additional tasks.** Although we see strong results for the above task clusters, one limitation with instruction tuning is that it does not improve performance for many language modeling tasks (e.g., commonsense reasoning or coreference resolution tasks formulated as sentence completions). For seven commonsense reasoning and coreference resolution tasks (see Table 2 in the Appendix), FLAN only outperforms LaMDA-PT on three of the seven tasks. This negative result indicates that when the downstream task is the same as the original language modeling pre-training objective (i.e., in cases where instructions are largely redundant), instruction tuning is not useful. Finally, we report results for sentiment analysis, paraphrase detection, and struct-to-text, as well as additional datasets for which GPT-3 results are not available, in Table 2 and Table 1 in the Appendix. Generally, zero-shot FLAN outperforms zero-shot LaMDA-PT and is comparable with or better than few-shot LaMDA-PT.

## 4 ABLATION STUDIES & FURTHER ANALYSIS

## 4.1 NUMBER OF INSTRUCTION TUNING CLUSTERS

As the core question of our paper asks how instruction tuning improves a model’s zero-shot performance on unseen tasks, in this first ablation we examine how performance is affected by the number of clusters and tasks used in instruction tuning. For this setup, we hold out NLI, closed-book QA, and commonsense reasoning as evaluation clusters, and use the seven remaining clusters for instruction tuning.[3] We show results for one to seven instruction tuning clusters, where clusters are added in decreasing order of number of tasks per cluster.

Figure 6 shows these results. As expected, we observe that average performance across the three held-out clusters improves as we add additional clusters and tasks to instruction tuning (with the exception of the sentiment analysis cluster), confirming the benefits of our proposed instruction tuning approach on zero-shot performance on novel tasks. It is further interesting to see that, for the seven clusters we test, the performance does not appear to saturate, implying that performance may further improve with even more clusters added to instruction tuning. Of note, this ablation does not allow us to draw conclusions about which instruction tuning cluster contributes the most to each evaluation cluster, although we see minimal added value from the sentiment analysis cluster.

## 4.2 SCALING LAWS

As Brown et al. (2020) shows that zero and few-shot capabilities of language models substantially improve for larger models, we next explore how the benefits of instruction tuning are affected by model scale. Using the same cluster split as in the previous ablation study, we evaluate the effect of instruction tuning on models of size 422M, 2B, 8B, 68B, and 137B parameters.

Figure 7 shows these results. The behavior on held-out tasks for the mixture of instruction tuning tasks. 8B and smaller models, however, is thoughtprovoking—instruction tuning actually hurts performance on held-out tasks. One potential explanation for this result could be that for small-scale models, learning the _∼_ 40 tasks used during instruction tuning fills the entire model capacity, causing these models to perform worse on new tasks. Under this potential explanation, for the larger scale models, instruction tuning fills up some model capacity but also teaches these models how to follow instructions, allowing them to generalize to new tasks with the remaining capacity.

## 4.3 ROLE OF INSTRUCTIONS

In a final ablation study, we explore the role of instructions during finetuning, as one possibility is that performance gains come entirely from multi-task finetuning and the model could perform just as well without instructions. We hence consider two finetuning setups without instructions. In a _no template_ setup, only inputs and outputs were given to the model (e.g., for translation the input would be “ _The dog runs._ ” and the output would be “ _Le chien court._ ”). In a _dataset name_ setup, each input is prepended with the name of the task and dataset (e.g., for translation to French, the input would be “ _[Translation: WMT’14 to French] The dog runs._ ”).

We compare these two ablations to FLAN’s finetuning procedure, which used natural instructions (e.g., “ _Please translate this sentence to French: ‘The dog runs.’_ ”). We perform evaluations for four held-out clus-

## 4.4 INSTRUCTIONS WITH FEW-SHOT EXEMPLARS

So far, we have focused on instruction tuning in the zero-shot setting. Here, we study how instruction tuning can be used when few-shot exemplars are available at inference time. The format for the few-shot setting builds on the zero-shot format. For some input _x_ and output _y_ , let instruct( _x_ ) denote the zero-shot instructions. Then, given _k_ few-shot exemplars ( _xi, yi_ ) _[k] i_ =1[and][a][new][input] _x_ , the instruction format for the few-shot setting is “instruct( _x_ 1) _⊕ y_ 1 _⊕_ instruct( _x_ 2) _⊕ y_ 2 _⊕ . . . ⊕_

7

instruct( _xk_ ) _⊕yk ⊕_ instruct( _x_ )”, where _⊕_ denotes string concatenation with a delimiter token inserted in between. At both training and inference time, exemplars are randomly drawn from the training set, and the number of exemplars is capped at 16 and such that the total sequence length is less than 960 tokens. Our experiment uses the same task splits and evaluation procedure as §3, such that few-shot exemplars for an unseen task are only used at inference time.

As shown in Figure 9, few-shot exemplars improve the performance on all task clusters, compared with zero-shot FLAN. Exemplars are especially effective for tasks with large/complex output spaces, such as struct to text, translation, and closed-book QA, potentially because exemplars help the model better understand the output format. In addition, for all task clusters, standard deviation among templates is lower for few-shot FLAN, indicating reduced sensitivity to prompt engineering.

As we’ve seen that instruction tuning improves the ability of a model to respond to instructions, it follows that, if FLAN is indeed more amenable to performing NLP tasks, then it should also achieve better performance when performing inference using soft prompts, represented by prepended continuous variables optimized via prompt tuning (Li & Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021). As further analysis, we train continuous prompts for each of the SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019a) tasks in accordance with the cluster splits from §2.2 such that when prompt-tuning on task _T_ , no tasks in the same cluster as _T_ were seen during instruction tuning. Our prompt tuning setup follows the procedure of Lester et al. (2021) except that we use a prompt length of 10, weight decay of 1e-4, and did not use dropout on the attention scores; we found in preliminary experiments that these changes improved the performance of LaMDA-PT.

We see that in all scenarios, prompt tuning works better with FLAN than LaMDA-PT. In many cases, especially for the low-resource setting, prompt tuning on FLAN even achieves more than 10% improvement over prompt tuning on the LaMDA-PT. This result exemplifies in another way how instruction tuning can result in a checkpoint that is more desirable for performing NLP tasks.

## 5 RELATED WORK

Our work relates to several broad research areas including zero-shot learning, prompting, multi-task learning, and language models for NLP applications (Radford et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Efrat & Levy, 2020; Aghajanyan et al., 2021; Li & Liang, 2021, _inter alia_ ). We describe prior work for these broad areas in an extended related work section (Appendix D), and here we describe two subareas narrower in scope that perhaps relate most closely to our work.

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

The way we ask a model to respond to instructions is similar to QA-based task formulation (Kumar et al., 2016; McCann et al., 2018), which aims to unify NLP tasks by casting them as QA over a context. Though these methods are very similar to ours, they mostly focus on multi-task learning instead of zero-shot learning, and—as noted by Liu et al. (2021)—they are generally not motivated by using existing knowledge in pretrained LMs. Moreover, our work supercedes recent work such as Chai et al. (2020) and Zhong et al. (2021) in terms of both model scale and scope of tasks.

The success of language models has led to nascent research on the ability of models to follow instructions. Most recently, Mishra et al. (2021) finetune 140M parameter BART on instructions with few-shot exemplars, and evaluate its few-shot abilities on unseen tasks—this is similar to our few-shot instruction tuning result from §4.4. This promising result (as well as one from Ye et al. (2021), which does not emphasize instructions as much) suggests that finetuning on a collection of tasks improves few-shot performance on unseen tasks, even at a smaller model scale. Sanh et al. (2021) finetune T5 in a setup similar to ours, finding that zero-shot learning can be improved in a model of 11B parameters. At a model scale similar to ours, OpenAI’s InstructGPT models are trained via both finetuning and reinforcement learning to produce outputs that are more preferred by human raters (Ouyang et al., 2022).

## 6 DISCUSSION

Our paper has explored a simple question in zero-shot prompting: does finetuning a model on a collection of tasks phrased as instructions improve its performance on unseen tasks? We operationalize this question via instruction tuning, a simple method that combines appealing aspects of both the pretrain–finetune and prompting paradigms. Our instruction-tuned model, FLAN, improves performance against an untuned model and surpasses zero-shot GPT-3 on the majority of tasks that we evaluate on. Ablation studies reveal that performance on unseen tasks improves with the number of instruction tuning task clusters, and, interestingly, that performance improvements from instruction tuning emerge only with sufficient model scale. Moreover, instruction tuning can be combined with other prompting methods such as few-shot prompting and prompt tuning.

The diverse capabilities of language models at scale have drawn attention to the tradeoffs between specialist models (one model per task) and generalist models (one model for many tasks; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Pratap et al., 2020), for which our study has potential implications. Although one might expect labeled data to have the most natural role in improving specialist models, instruction tuning demonstrates how labeled data can be used to help large language models perform many, unseen tasks. In other words, the positive effect of instruction tuning on cross-task generalization shows that task-specific training is complementary to general language modeling and motivates further research on generalist models.

As for limitations of our study, there is a degree of subjectivity in assigning tasks to clusters (though we try to use accepted categorizations in the literature), and we only explore the use of relatively short instructions of typically a single sentence (c.f. detailed instructions given to crowd-workers). A limitation for our evaluation is that individual examples might have appeared in the models’ pretraining data, which includes web documents, though in post-hoc analysis (Appendix C) we do not find any evidence that data overlap substantially impacted the results. Finally, the scale of FLAN 137B makes it costly to serve. Future work on instruction tuning could include gathering/generating even more task clusters for finetuning, cross-lingual experiments, using FLAN to generate data for training downstream classifiers, and using finetuning to improve model behavior with respect to bias and fairness (Solaiman & Dennison, 2021).

## 7 CONCLUSIONS

This paper has explored a simple method for improving the ability of language models at scale to perform zero-shot tasks based purely on instructions. Our instruction-tuned model, FLAN, compares favorably against GPT-3 and signals the potential ability for language models at scale to follow instructions. We hope that our paper will spur further research on instructions-based NLP, zero-shot learning, and using labeled data to improve large language models.

9

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## ETHICAL CONSIDERATIONS

This work uses language models, for which the risks and potential harms are discussed in Bender & Koller (2020), Brown et al. (2020), Bender et al. (2021), Patterson et al., (2021), and others. As our contribution in this paper is not a pretrained language model itself but rather an empirical study of how instruction tuning affects the zero-shot performance of a language model on unseen tasks, we additionally highlight two relevant ethical considerations. First, labeled datasets such as those we use for finetuning can contain undesirable biases, and these biases can be propagated into zero-shot applications of the model on downstream tasks. And second, instruction-tuned models can potentially require less data and expertise to use; such lower barriers to access could increase both the benefits and associated risks of such models.

## ENVIRONMENTAL CONSIDERATIONS

We use the same pretrained language models as Austin et al. (2021). The energy cost and carbon footprint for the pretrained models were 451 MWh and 26 tCO2e, respectively. The additional instruction tuning gradient-steps for finetuning FLAN is less than 2% of the number of pretraining steps, and so the estimated additional energy cost is comparatively smaller.

## AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS

Vincent Zhao and Kelvin Guu designed, implemented, and conducted the few-shot FLAN experiments. Maarten Bosma and Jason Wei ran the data contamination analysis. Brian Lester ran the prompt tuning experiments. Quoc V. Le and Andrew M. Dai advised, provided high-level guidance, and helped edit the paper.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces 'instruction tuning,' a method where large language models are finetuned on a wide variety of NLP tasks described through natural language instructions. The resulting model, FLAN, demonstrates significant improvements in zero-shot performance on unseen task clusters, often outperforming much larger models like GPT-3.

The paper makes a significant contribution by demonstrating that explicit supervision on a diverse set of tasks can unlock better zero-shot generalization in large-scale models. It identifies a critical scaling threshold where instruction tuning shifts from being detrimental to beneficial, which is a key insight for the field.

Overview issues:

- Unclear handling of potential data contamination: The authors acknowledge in Section 6 that evaluation data might have appeared in the pretraining set (LaMDA-PT), but the post-hoc analysis in Appendix C is not detailed in the main body. Given that zero-shot integrity is the paper's core claim, the lack of a rigorous overlap analysis between the 62 finetuning datasets and the 2.49T token pretraining corpus is a major gap. If the model saw 'unseen' test questions during its massive pretraining phase, the performance gains might reflect memorization rather than instruction following. The authors should include a summary of the overlap statistics and explain how they filtered or accounted for exact string matches between the test sets and the pretraining data.

- Ambiguity in 'unseen' task semantics: Section 2.2 defines an 'unseen task' as a dataset belonging to a held-out cluster, yet the semantic boundaries between these clusters are often porous. For example, 'Commonsense Reasoning' and 'Natural Language Inference' (NLI) both frequently require logical entailment and world knowledge. The paper does not sufficiently analyze whether the model is learning a general ability to follow instructions or if it is simply mapping new instructions to the most similar task seen during tuning. A more rigorous analysis would involve a similarity metric between task clusters to prove that performance on a held-out cluster is not just a function of its proximity to a seen cluster.

- Negative interference at smaller scales: The scaling law analysis in Section 4.2 reveals that instruction tuning actually degrades performance for models smaller than 8B parameters. The hypothesis that these models lack 'capacity' is plausible but underdeveloped. This result suggests a fundamental limitation of the method that is not fully explored—specifically, whether there is a way to regularize the finetuning process to prevent this degradation. The paper would be much stronger if it investigated whether the drop in performance is due to catastrophic forgetting of pretraining knowledge or an inability to parse the instruction templates themselves.

- Sensitivity to instruction template diversity: While Section 4.3 shows that instructions are better than no instructions, it does not isolate the effect of template variety versus the quality of a single 'best' instruction. The authors use 10 templates per dataset, but they don't show the variance in performance across these templates for the zero-shot tasks. If the model is highly sensitive to specific phrasing, the claim of 'zero-shot' utility is weakened for real-world users who may not phrase instructions like the training templates. Adding a breakdown of performance variance across the 10 templates for each held-out task would clarify the robustness of the instruction-following capability.

- Incomplete comparison with few-shot baselines: In Section 3, FLAN is compared to GPT-3's few-shot performance, but the comparison is sometimes inconsistent across datasets. For instance, FLAN outperforms few-shot GPT-3 on some tasks but lags on others like translation (Section 3, Translation). The paper lacks a deep discussion on why instruction tuning fails to bridge the gap for certain task types. Identifying the specific characteristics of tasks where few-shot prompting remains superior would provide a more balanced view of the method's limitations and help define the boundaries of zero-shot generalization.

- Missing analysis of instruction-tuning's impact on base pretraining capabilities: The paper demonstrates that instruction tuning improves zero-shot performance on NLP benchmarks, but it fails to measure whether this process degrades the model's original generative capabilities. Large language models are often valued for their open-ended creativity and broad world knowledge, yet finetuning on a narrow set of 60+ academic datasets can lead to 'alignment tax' or catastrophic forgetting. The authors should evaluate FLAN on a standard perplexity benchmark or a qualitative generation task, such as WikiText-103 or a subset of the pretraining data, to ensure that instruction following does not come at the cost of the model's fundamental linguistic modeling or knowledge retrieval. This is a standard requirement for papers proposing a new training paradigm for foundation models.

- Lack of evaluation on truly open-ended or 'wild' instructions: The paper claims the model is a 'zero-shot learner' via instructions, yet the evaluation is restricted to academic datasets that have been retrofitted with templates. There is a significant gap between following a template like 'Translate this sentence to French' and following a novel, multi-step instruction that does not resemble a standard NLP task (e.g., 'Write a poem about a robot in the style of Hemingway'). To support the claim of general instruction following, the authors should include a small-scale human evaluation or qualitative analysis on a set of 'out-of-distribution' instructions that do not map directly to the 12 task clusters. Without this, it is unclear if the model has learned to follow instructions or has simply learned to perform a 'mixture of experts' task classification.

- Missing discussion on the computational cost of the evaluation protocol: The methodology requires training $c$ separate models to evaluate $c$ task clusters, which is a massive computational undertaking for a 137B parameter model. While this ensures zero-shot integrity, the paper provides no guidance on the feasibility of this approach for researchers with fewer resources or how the 'held-out' models differ in their internal representations. The authors should provide a table comparing the training dynamics (e.g., loss curves or gradient norms) across at least two different cluster-held-out runs. This would clarify if the instruction-tuning process is stable across different task mixtures or if certain clusters (like NLI) are significantly harder for the model to adapt to than others.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Move the data contamination analysis summary from the Appendix to the main body to substantiate zero-shot claims.

- Add a per-template variance analysis for at least one major task cluster to demonstrate the model's sensitivity to instruction phrasing.

- Provide a more detailed discussion or a small experiment on why models below 8B parameters suffer from instruction tuning.

- Clarify the semantic overlap between task clusters to address concerns that 'unseen' tasks are too similar to 'seen' tasks.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Evaluation Protocol for Multi-Cluster Datasets
comments.quote: In this work, we only consider dataset  $\mathcal{D}$  unseen at evaluation time if no datasets from any task clusters that  $\mathcal{D}$  belongs to were seen during instruction tuning.
comments.explanation: The definition of an 'unseen task' creates a logical contradiction when a dataset belongs to multiple clusters. If dataset D belongs to both Cluster A and Cluster B, the text states it is only 'unseen' if no datasets from any task clusters it belongs to were seen. However, the subsequent sentence describes a leave-one-cluster-out protocol: 'we instruction tune c models, where each model holds out a different task cluster'. If a dataset belongs to two clusters, holding out only one cluster at a time would mean that dataset D is actually 'seen' during the evaluation of the other cluster it belongs to. To maintain the 'conservative definition' claimed, you must clarify if you held out all overlapping clusters simultaneously for such datasets or if no datasets in your study actually belong to multiple clusters.

comments.title: Potential label leakage in 'turned around' templates
comments.quote: While most of the ten templates describe the original task, to increase diversity, for each dataset we also include up to three templates that "turned the task around," (e.g., for sentiment classification we include templates asking to generate a movie review).
comments.explanation: The inclusion of 'turned around' templates creates a potential for label leakage during the instruction tuning process. If a model is trained to generate a movie review given a sentiment label (the 'turned around' task), it is effectively learning the conditional probability P(text|label). At inference time, when the model is evaluated on the original zero-shot task P(label|text), it can leverage the associations learned in the reverse direction. This is problematic because the paper claims to evaluate on 'unseen task types,' but the model has already been exposed to the semantic relationship between the specific labels and the text domain of that dataset during tuning. This makes the evaluation less of a pure zero-shot test of instruction following and more of a test of label-text association.

comments.title: Inconsistent mixing rate and total training steps
comments.quote: To balance the different sizes of datasets, we limit the number of training examples per dataset to 30k and follow the examples-proportional mixing scheme *(Raffel et al., 2020)* with a mixing rate maximum of 3k. We finetune all models for 30k gradient steps with a batch size of 8,192 tokens using the Adafactor Optimizer *(Shazeer and Stern, 2018)* with a learning rate of 3e-5.
comments.explanation: The text states that datasets are limited to 30k examples, but then specifies a mixing rate maximum of 3k. In the cited examples-proportional mixing scheme (Raffel et al., 2020), the 'maximum' parameter (K) typically defines the point at which a dataset is capped for the purpose of calculating its proportion in the mixture, not a secondary limit on the total examples. If K=3,000 and the total steps are 30,000 with a batch size of 8,192 tokens, the model processes approximately 245 million tokens. With over 60 datasets, a 3k cap on mixing proportions would mean the model sees far fewer than the 30k examples per dataset mentioned earlier. This creates a contradiction in the described data volume. Please clarify if the 30k limit is the absolute cap per dataset and the 3k is the proportional cap (K) used for sampling weights.

comments.title: Logical Flaw in Probability Mass Argument for Rank Classification
comments.quote: Though this procedure is logically sound, it is imperfect in that the probability mass for answers may have an undesired distribution among ways of saying each answer (e.g., a large number of alternative ways of saying “yes” may lower the probability mass assigned to “yes”).
comments.explanation: The argument that alternative ways of saying 'yes' lower the probability of the specific token 'yes' is a misunderstanding of how rank classification (as defined in Brown et al. 2020) works. In rank classification, the model's likelihood is compared only across the provided candidate strings (e.g., 'yes' vs 'no'). The existence of other high-probability strings like 'correct' or 'true' does not change the relative ranking of 'yes' and 'no' unless those strings are explicitly included in the candidate set. The 'OPTIONS' suffix actually introduces a new potential bias: it forces the model to allocate probability mass to a specific format, which might conflict with the model's natural distribution if the suffix itself is not well-represented in the pretraining data.

comments.title: Inconsistent evaluation protocol for no-template ablation
comments.quote: For the no template setup, we used the FLAN instructions during zero-shot inference (because if we used no template, the model would not know what task to perform).
comments.explanation: There is a logical mismatch between the training and inference conditions in this ablation. The model is trained on raw input-output pairs but is then evaluated using natural language instructions that it never encountered during the finetuning phase. This creates a distribution shift where the model might fail not because it lacks task knowledge, but because it has been conditioned to expect bare inputs and is now confused by the instruction text. To properly isolate the role of instructions, you should have also evaluated the 'no template' model using a few-shot 'no template' prompt to establish a baseline for its task performance without the distribution shift.

comments.title: Incomplete carbon footprint for multi-model evaluation
comments.quote: The additional instruction tuning gradient-steps for finetuning FLAN is less than 2% of the number of pretraining steps, and so the estimated additional energy cost is comparatively smaller.
comments.explanation: Section 2.2 states that to evaluate zero-shot FLAN on c task clusters, you 'instruction tune c models.' This means the total energy cost for the results presented in the paper is not just the cost of one FLAN model, but the sum of all c models trained for the leave-one-cluster-out evaluation. The current environmental statement implies a single tuning run, which contradicts the evaluation methodology. The total carbon footprint should reflect the aggregate cost of all models trained to produce the paper's main results.

comments.title: Sequence length and packing mismatch
comments.quote: The input and target sequence lengths used in finetuning are 1024 and 256, respectively. We use packing *(Raffel et al., 2020)* to combine multiple training examples into a single sequence, separating inputs from targets using a special EOS token.
comments.explanation: The paper specifies a decoder-only architecture (LaMDA-PT) but describes separate input (1024) and target (256) sequence lengths. In a standard decoder-only transformer using packing, the model processes a single contiguous sequence of tokens where the loss is masked for the input portion. Specifying two distinct lengths suggests an encoder-decoder setup or a fixed-split packing strategy that is not standard for dense decoders. If the total sequence length is 1024, the target cannot be an additional 256 tokens without exceeding the model's positional embedding limit or memory allocation. Please clarify the total sequence length and how the 1024/256 split is enforced within the packed decoder-only sequences.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for instruction function
comments.quote: For some input  $x$  and output  $y$ , let  $\text{instruct}(x)$  denote the zero-shot instructions. Then, given  $k$  few-shot exemplars  $(x_i, y_i)_{i=1}^k$  and a new input  $x$ , the instruction format for the few-shot setting is "instruct  $(x_1) \oplus y_1 \oplus \text{instruct}(x_2) \oplus y_2 \oplus \ldots \oplus$
comments.explanation: The text introduces the function as instruct(x) in the first sentence, but then uses the word 'instruct' as a literal string or a different function name in the subsequent sequence definition: 'instruct (x1)'. This creates ambiguity as to whether 'instruct' is a function applied to x or a prefix string. If it is a function, the notation should be consistent throughout the sequence. If it is a string, it contradicts the definition of instruct(x) as the zero-shot instructions.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper makes a clear and impactful contribution by demonstrating that instruction tuning—finetuning on diverse NLP tasks phrased as natural language instructions—can dramatically improve zero-shot generalization in large language models. The core idea is simple yet powerful, and the empirical results are strong across multiple task families. The ablation studies on model scale, instruction format, and task diversity add credibility to the central claim. This work helped establish a foundational paradigm in instruction-following models.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient justification for task clustering and potential leakage: The paper relies on task clusters to define 'unseen' tasks during evaluation, but the rationale for how datasets are grouped into clusters is not rigorously justified. For example, translation and sentiment analysis are treated as distinct clusters, yet both involve language understanding and generation patterns that could generalize across tasks. More critically, some datasets used in instruction tuning may share underlying sources or formulations with evaluation datasets—for instance, multiple QA datasets drawing from Wikipedia or similar knowledge sources—raising the risk of unintentional data leakage. This undermines the claim of true zero-shot generalization. The post-hoc contamination analysis mentioned in the discussion is insufficient without quantitative reporting or exclusion criteria. The authors should either provide a principled taxonomy of task differences or include a more detailed analysis ruling out overlap in topics, domains, or surface forms between tuning and evaluation sets.

- Overstatement of generalization beyond verbalizable tasks: The paper claims broad zero-shot learning improvements, but Figure 5 and Section 3 show that instruction tuning fails on tasks where the instruction format adds little value—such as sentence completion in coreference or commonsense reasoning. The authors acknowledge this limitation but do not fully integrate it into their framing. Readers may walk away with an inflated view of FLAN's generality, when in fact its success is tightly linked to tasks that naturally admit question-answer or directive phrasing. This selective reporting of successes risks overhyping the method's scope. The paper would benefit from upfront qualification that instruction tuning is most effective when the target task can be clearly verbalized as an instruction, and less so when it aligns too closely with pretraining objectives.

- Ablation study lacks isolation of instruction quality effects: The ablation in Section 4.3 compares instruction tuning with no-template and dataset-name baselines, concluding that natural language instructions are crucial. However, this comparison conflates two factors: the presence of instructions versus their linguistic quality or clarity. The 'dataset name' setup (e.g., '[Translation: WMT'14 to French]') is not a clean control—it may simply be less intelligible than natural instructions. A stronger ablation would include a version with templated but non-natural phrasing (e.g., 'Task 12: map input to output') to isolate whether performance gains stem from natural language per se or just structured task signaling. Without this, the claim that 'natural language' is key remains undersupported; it may be that any consistent task delimiter helps, not necessarily human-readable phrasing.

- Evaluation metrics do not account for prompt variance: The paper reports mean performance across up to 10 templates per dataset, but does not adequately address the variance across prompts. High variance could indicate that performance depends more on lucky prompt phrasing than robust generalization. While Figure 8 in the ablation hints at this by showing standard deviation, the main results downplay it. For a zero-shot method claiming to eliminate the need for prompt engineering, this sensitivity matters. The authors should report confidence intervals or interquartile ranges alongside means, and discuss whether FLAN truly reduces reliance on prompt tuning or merely shifts the burden to the finetuning stage, where template design already occurred.

- Lack of comparison to concurrent instruction-tuning baselines: The paper positions FLAN as a novel contribution but omits direct comparison to T0 (Sanh et al., 2022), a contemporaneous model that also applied multitask instruction tuning to T5 and demonstrated zero-shot transfer. While T0 used a smaller model (11B), its methodology is nearly identical, and citing it only in related work without empirical comparison weakens the novelty claim. Given that both models use instruction tuning on diverse NLP tasks, a side-by-side evaluation—even on a subset of tasks—would strengthen the paper’s impact. The absence suggests the authors may be overstating FLAN’s uniqueness when the core idea was emerging simultaneously across groups.

- No evidence that unseen tasks are truly out-of-distribution: The paper defines 'unseen' tasks at the cluster level but does not rule out domain or topic overlap between instruction tuning and evaluation sets. For instance, multiple QA datasets (e.g., NQ, TriviaQA) draw from Wikipedia, raising the risk that models memorize facts rather than learn task-agnostic instruction following. This undermines the claim of zero-shot generalization. To validate true out-of-distribution performance, the authors should report a detailed contamination analysis—specifically, compute the overlap in entity mentions or n-grams between instruction tuning examples and evaluation datasets. Without this, the central claim of cross-task generalization remains vulnerable to alternative explanations based on data leakage.

- Missing direct comparison to T0 despite identical methodology: The paper presents FLAN as a novel contribution but does not compare it to T0 (Sanh et al., 2021), a contemporaneous model that applied the same core idea—multitask instruction tuning—to T5. Both models use natural language instructions and evaluate zero-shot transfer, making a side-by-side comparison essential for assessing novelty. The absence of even a qualitative or subset-based benchmark suggests the paper overstates FLAN's uniqueness. A minimal fix would be to evaluate T0's public checkpoint on a shared subset of tasks like RTE or BoolQ and include the results in Figure 5, allowing readers to judge relative gains.

- No analysis of whether instruction tuning aids prompt tuning across tasks: Section 4.5 shows that FLAN responds better to soft prompts than LaMDA-PT, but the experiment is limited to SuperGLUE. The paper misses the chance to demonstrate broader implications by not testing whether instruction tuning improves prompt tuning efficiency—such as faster convergence or lower variance—across diverse task clusters. To strengthen this point, the authors should report the number of optimization steps or learning curves for prompt tuning on at least two non-SuperGLUE tasks (e.g., translation or closed-book QA), showing that instruction tuning creates a more adaptable representation beyond a single benchmark.

- Failure to test generalization to non-NLP tasks or formats: The paper claims broad zero-shot learning abilities but only evaluates on standard NLP benchmarks. A complete demonstration would include at least one task outside traditional NLP—such as code generation or mathematical reasoning—where instruction following is non-trivial and not implicitly covered in pretraining. For example, evaluating FLAN on a held-out subset of HumanEval or MATH would show whether instruction tuning enables reasoning in novel domains. Without such a test, the scope of 'zero-shot learning' appears confined to rephrased NLP tasks, limiting the perceived impact.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a principled justification for task clustering and include a quantitative analysis ruling out data overlap between instruction tuning and evaluation sets.

- Reframe the claims about generalization to acknowledge that instruction tuning is most effective for tasks that are naturally verbalizable, and less so for language modeling-style tasks.

- Improve the ablation study by isolating the effect of natural language phrasing from structured task signaling, possibly with a control using non-natural but systematic templates.

- Report and discuss variance in performance across instruction templates to assess robustness to prompt formulation.

- Include a direct comparison with T0 or similar contemporaneous instruction-tuning work to properly position FLAN’s novelty and performance.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of few-shot GPT-3 outperformance
comments.quote: FLAN even outperforms few-shot GPT-3 by a large margin on ANLI, RTE, BoolQ, AI2-ARC, OpenbookQA, and StoryCloze.
comments.explanation: The claim that FLAN outperforms 'few-shot GPT-3 by a large margin' lacks context. The few-shot performance of GPT-3 on these tasks is not reported, nor is the number of shots or prompt design specified. Without this information, the magnitude and fairness of the comparison are unclear. The statement should be qualified with details on shot count and prompting strategy, or revised to avoid overstating the result.

comments.title: Inconsistent dataset count in evaluation
comments.quote: FLAN substantially improves the performance of its unmodified counterpart and surpasses zero-shot 175B GPT-3 on 20 of 25 datasets that we evaluate.
comments.explanation: The abstract states evaluation on 25 datasets, but the figure caption mentions only ten task types. This discrepancy is not explained. If 'task types' are broader categories, this should be clarified. Otherwise, the numbers conflict. Add a sentence to reconcile the counts: 'These 25 datasets span ten distinct task types, grouped by semantic and functional similarity.'

comments.title: Misleading comparison to GPT-3 without size context
comments.quote: FLAN's zero-shot also outperforms 175B-parameter GPT-3's zero-shot on 20 of 25 datasets that we evaluate, and even outperforms GPT-3's few-shot by a large margin on ANLI, RTE, BoolQ, AI2-ARC, OpenbookQA, and StoryCloze.
comments.explanation: The comparison to GPT-3 is meaningful but risks overstating FLAN's advantage by not contextualizing model size. FLAN is 137B, while GPT-3 is 175B—larger models typically perform better. The paper notes that instruction tuning benefits emerge only at scale, yet does not rule out size as a confounding factor. Clarify that gains are relative to a larger model, and thus reflect both method and scale effects.

comments.title: Ambiguity in template diversity claim
comments.quote: For each dataset, we manually compose ten unique templates that use natural language instructions to describe the task for that dataset. While most of the ten templates describe the original task, to increase diversity, for each dataset we also include up to three templates that "turned the task around," (e.g., for sentiment classification we include templates asking to generate a movie review).
comments.explanation: The claim that templates 'describe the original task' while including some that 'turned the task around' is contradictory. A template asking to generate a movie review given a sentiment label does not test sentiment classification but instead tests controllable generation. This introduces a hidden task shift not accounted for in evaluation. Clarify that some templates are for inverse or auxiliary tasks and thus do not preserve the original task structure.

comments.title: Unclear status of 'turned around' templates in training
comments.quote: to increase diversity, for each dataset we also include up to three templates that "turned the task around," (e.g., for sentiment classification we include templates asking to generate a movie review). We then instruction tune a pretrained language model on the mixture of all datasets, with examples in each dataset formatted via a randomly selected instruction template for that dataset.
comments.explanation: It is not specified whether 'turned around' templates involve swapping inputs and outputs or just rephrasing. If a sentiment example uses a template asking to generate a review but keeps the label as 'positive', the supervision is inconsistent. Conversely, if inputs and outputs are swapped, it becomes a different task. Clarify how examples are formatted when such templates are used—specifically, whether input and output roles are reversed.

comments.title: Misleading implication of task homogeneity within clusters
comments.quote: Figure 3 shows these datasets—each dataset is categorized into one of twelve task clusters, for which datasets in a given cluster are of the same task type.
comments.explanation: The statement that datasets in a cluster are 'of the same task type' is too strong. For example, grouping multiple QA datasets ignores differences in format (extractive vs. open-domain) and context use. This assumption weakens the zero-shot claim, as clusters may not represent truly distinct task types. Revise to reflect that clusters are broad categories: 'datasets in a given cluster share a general task type but may differ in formulation or domain.'

comments.title: Circular evaluation design undermines zero-shot claim
comments.quote: Hence, to evaluate zero-shot FLAN on  $c$  task clusters, we instruction tune  $c$  models, where each model holds out a different task cluster for evaluation.
comments.explanation: Training one model per held-out cluster creates a circular setup: each model is specifically trained to exclude the evaluation cluster, meaning the evaluation anticipates the test domain. True zero-shot learning should generalize to tasks entirely outside the training distribution. This design effectively enables interpolation rather than generalization. A single model trained on a fixed set of clusters should be evaluated on clusters never seen during any training phase.

comments.title: Options suffix introduces evaluation-time leakage
comments.quote: Therefore, we include an options suffix, in which we append the token OPTIONS to the end of a classification task along with a list of the output classes for that task.
comments.explanation: Appending an explicit list of output classes at inference leaks label information not present in standard zero-shot setups. This turns classification into a constrained generation problem where the model can exploit the known label space. For example, listing 'entailment, contradiction, neutral' gives direct access to the answer set. This undermines claims of natural generalization. Either justify this as a necessary adaptation or compare against a baseline without the suffix.

comments.title: Unclear dataset balancing mechanism
comments.quote: To balance the different sizes of datasets, we limit the number of training examples per dataset to 30k and follow the examples-proportional mixing scheme *(Raffel et al., 2020)* with a mixing rate maximum of 3k.
comments.explanation: The description combines two mixing strategies without clarity. Limiting datasets to 30k examples and applying examples-proportional mixing with a 'mixing rate maximum of 3k' is ambiguous—does this mean 3k examples per dataset per step, per epoch, or total? The term 'mixing rate' is undefined in context. This undermines reproducibility. Clarify whether 'mixing rate maximum of 3k' refers to examples per dataset per gradient step or per epoch, and how it interacts with the 30k cap.

comments.title: Inconsistent token count with batch size and steps
comments.quote: We finetune all models for 30k gradient steps with a batch size of 8,192 tokens using the Adafactor Optimizer *(Shazeer and Stern, 2018)* with a learning rate of 3e-5. The input and target sequence lengths used in finetuning are 1024 and 256, respectively.
comments.explanation: The total tokens processed (30k steps × 8,192 tokens = ~246M) appear inconsistent with the data volume. Each example uses up to 1,280 tokens (1024 + 256), and with 60+ datasets capped at 30k examples, total data could reach ~2.3B tokens—far exceeding the processed amount. This suggests either the batch size is in sequences, not tokens, or the 30k cap is not enforced. Clarify whether 'batch size of 8,192 tokens' is accurate and reconcile it with the total data volume.

comments.title: Inconsistent evaluation protocol for GLaM comparison
comments.quote: With the best dev-template, zero-shot FLAN outperforms zero-shot GLaM on 13 of 19 available datasets and one-shot GLaM on 11 of 19 datasets.
comments.explanation: The comparison uses 'best dev-template' selection for FLAN but does not specify whether GLaM results are based on the same protocol. If GLaM is evaluated with mean performance or a different prompt setup, the comparison is unfair. This inflates FLAN's apparent gains. Either report GLaM's performance using best-template selection or use mean performance for both models to ensure parity.

comments.title: Misleading performance claim due to selective reporting
comments.quote: With the best dev template, zero-shot FLAN outperforms zero-shot GPT-3 on 20 of 25 datasets and even surpasses GPT-3’s few-shot performance on 10 datasets.
comments.explanation: Highlighting success on 20 of 25 datasets omits the magnitude of gains and losses. A model could win narrowly on 20 and lose badly on 5, resulting in worse overall performance. Without aggregate metrics (e.g., average accuracy), the claim risks misleading readers. Report mean performance across datasets and clarify whether the 10 datasets where FLAN beats few-shot GPT-3 are a subset of the 20.

comments.title: Contradiction in baseline suitability claim
comments.quote: For comparison, we report zero and few-shot results for LaMDA-PT using the same prompts as GPT-3 (as LaMDA-PT is not suitable for natural instructions without instruction tuning).
comments.explanation: The justification assumes untuned LaMDA-PT cannot handle natural instructions, yet the premise of instruction tuning is that such models *can* learn to follow them. This creates a contradiction. By restricting LaMDA-PT to GPT-3-style prompts, the baseline may be artificially weakened. A fairer ablation would test LaMDA-PT on the same instruction templates used for FLAN, even if performance is low.

comments.title: Evaluation clusters overlap with instruction tuning data sources
comments.quote: We hold out NLI, closed-book QA, and commonsense reasoning as evaluation clusters, and use the seven remaining clusters for instruction tuning. We show results for one to seven instruction tuning clusters, where clusters are added in decreasing order of number of tasks per cluster.
comments.explanation: The held-out clusters (e.g., NQ, TriviaQA) draw from Wikipedia, as do many instruction tuning datasets (e.g., SQuAD, WebQuestions). This raises the risk of factual memorization rather than true task-agnostic generalization. Without quantifying overlap in topics or entities, the observed gains could reflect data contamination. Add a contamination analysis to rule out significant content overlap between tuning and evaluation sets.

comments.title: Order of cluster addition introduces confounding
comments.quote: We show results for one to seven instruction tuning clusters, where clusters are added in decreasing order of number of tasks per cluster.
comments.explanation: Adding clusters by task count confounds cluster diversity with data volume. Performance gains may reflect more training examples rather than broader task coverage. For example, adding a cluster with 20 tasks provides more data than one with 5. To isolate the effect of diversity, control for task count or randomize the order of cluster addition across runs.

comments.title: Inconsistent task count in scaling analysis
comments.quote: One potential explanation for this result could be that for small-scale models, learning the  $\sim 40$  tasks used during instruction tuning fills the entire model capacity, causing these models to perform worse on new tasks.
comments.explanation: The section refers to '~40' tasks, but the abstract states 'over 60 NLP datasets.' This discrepancy is not addressed. If the scaling ablation used a subset, that should be explicitly stated. Otherwise, the inconsistency undermines confidence in the experimental setup. Revise to reflect the actual number of tasks used in the scaling experiments.

comments.title: Causal claim lacks supporting evidence
comments.quote: Under this potential explanation, for the larger scale models, instruction tuning fills up some model capacity but also teaches these models how to follow instructions, allowing them to generalize to new tasks with the remaining capacity.
comments.explanation: The text presents a causal mechanism—larger models learn to follow instructions while retaining capacity—but provides no direct evidence. The data show correlation, not causation. Smaller models might fail due to overfitting or lack of depth, not capacity saturation. Reframe as speculative: 'This pattern is consistent with the hypothesis that...' rather than stating it as a functional account.

comments.title: Inconsistent inference-time template usage
comments.quote: For the no template setup, we used the FLAN instructions during zero-shot inference (because if we used no template, the model would not know what task to perform). For models finetuned on dataset name only, we report zero-shot performance for FLAN instructions as well as using the dataset name.
comments.explanation: The evaluation is asymmetric: the no-template model is tested with FLAN's natural language instructions—formatting it never saw during training—while FLAN itself was trained and tested with such prompts. This gives the ablation model an unfair advantage. A fair comparison would test each model using the same format used during training.

comments.title: Incorrect few-shot formatting in equation
comments.quote: $\mathrm{instruct}(x_k) \oplus y_k \oplus \mathrm{instruct}(x)$ , where  $\oplus$  denotes string concatenation with a delimiter token inserted in between.
comments.explanation: The few-shot format applies `instruct(·)` to each exemplar input separately, resulting in redundant and potentially inconsistent task descriptions. Standard few-shot prompting uses a single instruction followed by raw (input, output) pairs. The correct format should be: instruct(x) ⊕ x₁ ⊕ y₁ ⊕ x₂ ⊕ y₂ ⊕ … ⊕ x_k ⊕ y_k. Rewrite to reflect standard practice and ensure coherence.

comments.title: Misleading standard deviation interpretation
comments.quote: In addition, for all task clusters, standard deviation among templates is lower for few-shot FLAN, indicating reduced sensitivity to prompt engineering.
comments.explanation: Lower standard deviation across templates suggests more consistent performance with different instructions, but it does not imply reduced sensitivity to prompt engineering overall. The model now depends on exemplar selection, which introduces a new source of variability. Without evidence that performance is stable across different exemplar sets, the claim is unsubstantiated. Clarify that sensitivity may be shifted from instruction wording to example choice.

comments.title: Claim of 10% improvement lacks quantitative support
comments.quote: In many cases, especially for the low-resource setting, prompt tuning on FLAN even achieves more than  $10\%$  improvement over prompt tuning on the LaMDA-PT.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'more than 10% improvement' is not substantiated. The text and Figure 10 do not provide numerical values to confirm whether gains exceed 10 percentage points. The phrasing also risks confusing absolute and relative improvements. Either report exact scores or remove the specific percentage claim.

comments.title: Evaluation setup contradicts stated cluster splits
comments.quote: As further analysis, we train continuous prompts for each of the SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019a) tasks in accordance with the cluster splits from §2.2 such that when prompt-tuning on task  $\mathcal{T}$ , no tasks in the same cluster as  $\mathcal{T}$  were seen during instruction tuning.
comments.explanation: This claim likely does not hold for all SuperGLUE tasks. For example, BoolQ is a QA task, and multiple QA datasets were included in instruction tuning. If QA is a cluster, then evaluating on BoolQ violates the unseen-cluster condition. The paper does not define cluster membership for SuperGLUE tasks, making verification impossible. Either define the cluster assignments or revise the claim to acknowledge potential overlap.

comments.title: Overstates novelty over prior work
comments.quote: *Sanh et al. (2021)* finetune T5 in a setup similar to ours, finding that zero-shot learning can be improved in a model of 11B parameters.
comments.explanation: This characterization understates T0, which pioneered multitask instruction tuning with natural language prompts and demonstrated zero-shot transfer across benchmarks. Presenting it as a minor result downplays its methodological impact. Acknowledge T0 as a key contemporary work that established the feasibility of the approach FLAN scales up.

comments.title: Incorrectly positions FLAN relative to InstructGPT
comments.quote: At a model scale similar to ours, OpenAI’s InstructGPT models are trained via both finetuning and reinforcement learning to produce outputs that are more preferred by human raters *(Ouyang et al., 2022)*.
comments.explanation: The statement implies InstructGPT is distinguished mainly by RLHF, but omits that it also uses supervised instruction finetuning—a core component shared with FLAN. This framing suggests FLAN’s approach is more novel than it is. Clarify that both models rely on instruction tuning, with InstructGPT adding RLHF for alignment.

comments.title: Overstates generalization beyond task clusters
comments.quote: Our instruction-tuned model, FLAN, improves performance against an untuned model and surpasses zero-shot GPT-3 on the majority of tasks that we evaluate on.
comments.explanation: The claim implies broad zero-shot transfer, but the evaluation only ensures cluster-level separation. It does not rule out domain or topic overlap (e.g., Wikipedia-based QA tasks). Without contamination analysis, the assertion risks conflating cluster novelty with true out-of-distribution generalization. Clarify that improvements are observed on tasks from unseen clusters, not necessarily on semantically unrelated tasks.

comments.title: Insufficient support for instruction tuning scalability claim
comments.quote: Ablation studies reveal that performance on unseen tasks improves with the number of instruction tuning task clusters, and, interestingly, that performance improvements from instruction tuning emerge only with sufficient model scale.
comments.explanation: The claim that benefits 'emerge only' at sufficient scale is too strong. The data show gains increase with scale, but do not demonstrate a phase transition or threshold. Without showing performance curves or capacity analysis, 'only' overstates the evidence. Rephrase to: 'performance gains increase with model scale' to better reflect the findings.

comments.title: Misleading implication about data contamination
comments.quote: A limitation for our evaluation is that individual examples might have appeared in the models’ pretraining data, which includes web documents, though in post-hoc analysis (Appendix C) we do not find any evidence that data overlap substantially impacted the results.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'do not find any evidence' is not equivalent to demonstrating absence of contamination. Without reporting metrics like n-gram overlap or entity recall, this negative finding cannot justify the conclusions. Given that even small overlaps can inflate performance, this limitation should not be downplayed. State: 'We cannot fully rule out contamination between pretraining and evaluation data, which may affect zero-shot performance estimates.'

comments.title: Overstates FLAN's comparison to GPT-3
comments.quote: Our instruction-tuned model, FLAN, compares favorably against GPT-3 and signals the potential ability for language models at scale to follow instructions.
comments.explanation: The claim that FLAN 'compares favorably' is overly broad. Results show gains on many tasks but not all—especially on tasks aligned with pretraining objectives. The conclusion presents the comparison as uniformly positive, which mischaracterizes the conditional nature of the improvement. Revise to: 'FLAN outperforms zero-shot and few-shot GPT-3 on many, but not all, unseen tasks.'

comments.title: Implies broad instruction-following ability without qualification
comments.quote: signals the potential ability for language models at scale to follow instructions.
comments.explanation: This statement suggests a universal advance in instruction following, but the paper's own results show gains are largely confined to tasks that admit natural question-answer formulations. Tasks like coreference resolution see little improvement. Qualify the claim to reflect the evidence: 'signals the potential for language models at scale to follow instructions on tasks that admit natural question-answer formulations.'

comments.title: Energy estimate lacks model scale specification
comments.quote: We use the same pretrained language models as *Austin et al. (2021)*. The energy cost and carbon footprint for the pretrained models were 451 MWh and 26 tCO2e, respectively.
comments.explanation: The energy and emissions figures are cited without confirming they correspond to the 137B model used. Energy consumption varies significantly with model size. Without this alignment, the environmental impact numbers may be misapplied. Specify that the figures are for the 137B model or cite the exact configuration from Austin et al. (2021).

comments.title: Invalid energy comparison due to missing normalization
comments.quote: The additional instruction tuning gradient-steps for finetuning FLAN is less than 2% of the number of pretraining steps, and so the estimated additional energy cost is comparatively smaller.
comments.explanation: The argument assumes energy scales linearly with step count, ignoring differences in batch size, sequence length, and hardware efficiency. Without reporting actual FLOPs or energy per step, the 2% claim does not justify 'comparatively smaller' energy. Either compute the actual estimated energy or qualify the statement with assumptions.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces FLAN, an instruction-tuned 137B parameter language model that demonstrates substantial improvements in zero-shot performance on unseen NLP tasks compared to both the base model and GPT-3. The methodology involves finetuning on 60+ datasets using natural language instructions, with evaluation on held-out task clusters to ensure true zero-shot testing. While the empirical results are impressive and the ablation studies are comprehensive, there are several important methodological concerns that need to be addressed regarding task selection, template design, evaluation protocols, and the generalizability of findings.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by demonstrating that instruction tuning can significantly improve zero-shot generalization in large language models. The experimental setup is generally sound, with careful attention to preventing data contamination through cluster-based evaluation splits. The ablation studies on scaling laws, the role of instructions, and the number of task clusters provide useful insights. However, the paper has some limitations in its methodology, particularly around the subjectivity of task clustering and template design, that should be addressed to strengthen the claims.

Overview issues:

- Task Clustering Subjectivity and Potential Leakage: The paper's evaluation relies on grouping datasets into 12 task clusters (Figure 3) and holding out entire clusters for evaluation. However, the authors acknowledge there's "a degree of subjectivity in assigning tasks to clusters." This subjectivity could significantly impact the claimed zero-shot performance. For instance, NLI, reading comprehension, and closed-book QA might share underlying linguistic patterns despite being in different clusters. The paper needs to provide more rigorous justification for these cluster assignments or conduct sensitivity analysis showing how performance changes with alternative clusterings. Without this, it's difficult to rule out that the improvements come from task similarity rather than true generalization to unseen task types.

- Template Design Quality and Consistency Issues: The methodology uses manually composed instruction templates (10 per dataset) but lacks systematic evaluation of template quality. The paper states that templates include up to three that "turned the task around," which introduces inconsistency in how tasks are presented. There's no analysis of whether different templates lead to systematically different performance levels, nor any inter-annotator agreement metrics for template quality. This is concerning because the results report averaging across templates, but if some templates are poorly designed, they could artificially depress performance. The paper should include an analysis of template variance and demonstrate that the improvements aren't driven by a subset of high-quality templates.

- Insufficient Statistical Significance Testing: The paper reports numerous performance comparisons (e.g., FLAN vs GPT-3 on 25 datasets) but provides no statistical significance testing. Given the variability in model performance across different prompts and templates, it's essential to establish whether the reported improvements are statistically meaningful. For example, when claiming FLAN "surpasses zero-shot 175B GPT-3 on 20 of 25 datasets," readers need confidence intervals or p-values to understand the reliability of these differences. The paper should either add statistical testing or clarify that the comparisons are point estimates without statistical guarantees.

- Limited Analysis of Template-Task Interaction Effects: The evaluation averages performance across templates for each dataset, but this masks potential important interactions between template styles and task types. The paper shows that instruction tuning works better for tasks "naturally verbalized as instructions" (like NLI) versus language modeling tasks, but doesn't systematically analyze how template design affects this pattern. A deeper analysis is needed to understand whether certain template formats work better for certain task types, which would be valuable for guiding future instruction tuning efforts. This analysis is particularly important given the finding that instruction tuning hurts performance on smaller models.
